A warm front moved slowly north into portions of northern Indiana as low pressure tracked across northwestern Indiana. Steep lapse rates and up to 1000 J/KG flowed into the area allowing for a few storms to become locally severe. Storms struggled to maintain their updrafts, resulting in mainly reports of small hail. One or two storms were able to produce marginally severe hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought heavy, wet snow to southeast portions of central Indiana. The heaviest of the snow fell across portions of Lawrence and Decatur counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CBS4 relayed a snow amount of 6.0 inches in the Greensburg area. No other impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought heavy, wet snow to southeast portions of central Indiana. The heaviest of the snow fell across portions of Lawrence and Decatur counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS report of 7.4 inches of snow was reported four miles east of Mitchell. Additionally, a CoCoRaHS report of 6.8 inches of snow was reported five miles northeast of Mitchell. No other impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed ahead of an area of low pressure and an upper level trough. Plentiful moisture combined with strong frontogenetical forcing produced an narrow area of heavy snow. To the southwest of the heavy snow, rain fell along with a few thunderstorms. To the northeast of the snow band, very dry air caused a sharp cut-off to the snow. A narrow band of intense snow produced amounts of 8 to 12 inches, with some locations receiving over 2 inches per hour. The 10.2 inches of snow at Indianapolis set the daily snowfall record and became the second highest one day total for the month of March. Visibility at the Indianapolis International Airport was a quarter of a mile or less from about 2:30 PM to 4:30 PM EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 10.5 inches was reported from Marion County. The highest amount reported came in from one mile north-northeast of Clermont at 10.5 inches. Owing partially to the event being well forecast and on a Saturday, no other impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed ahead of an area of low pressure and an upper level trough. Plentiful moisture combined with strong frontogenetical forcing produced an narrow area of heavy snow. To the southwest of the heavy snow, rain fell along with a few thunderstorms. To the northeast of the snow band, very dry air caused a sharp cut-off to the snow. A narrow band of intense snow produced amounts of 8 to 12 inches, with some locations receiving over 2 inches per hour. The 10.2 inches of snow at Indianapolis set the daily snowfall record and became the second highest one day total for the month of March. Visibility at the Indianapolis International Airport was a quarter of a mile or less from about 2:30 PM to 4:30 PM EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 13 inches was reported from Hendricks County. The highest amount reported came in from three miles south of Brownsburg at 12.6 inches. Owing partially to the event being well forecast and on a Saturday, no other impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell to between minus 10 and minus 15 degrees during the morning hours. After a morning low of 2 above zero, the actual high temperature at Evansville was only 11 above zero. Arctic high pressure centered over the northern Plains produced these dangerously cold wind chills.

Unseasonably high amounts of moisture flowed into central Indiana from the southwest and south during the latter part of February. At times during the period of February 19th to the 24th, the precipitable water values (a measure of moisture in the atmosphere) reached record levels for February.||Heavy rainfall fell early in the week (February 19/20) across northern sections of central Indiana. The second round of heavy rain on the 24th fell across southern sections of the area. In all, 7-day totals ending the morning of February 25th were from 3 to 6 inches across much of central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported County Road 800 North closed from County Road 100 East to County Road 200 East due to high water from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure at the surface tracked towards West Virginia. At the same time, a strong upper level low pressure system strengthened as it crossed east through Kentucky. The combination of the two and an onslaught of colder air brought significant snow accumulations to areas east of Wilmington, Ohio, with light accumulations found along and west of the I-75 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer located 6 miles south of Connersville measured 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front and a deep low pressure system brought widespread precipitation to southern Indiana January 12. As temperatures dropped from the 40s and 50s into the 30s and 20s, rain changed over to freezing rain, sleet, and snow. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 5 inches, which fell on top a glaze to 1/4 inch of ice. Travel was severely impacted with widespread school and business closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of snow fell on top of 0.1 to 0.2 inches of ice accumulation across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration lake effect snow event created difficult travel conditions January 3rd through January 6th. Three day snowfall totals ranged between 1 and 2 feet across portions of La Porte and Saint Joseph counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy lake effect snow developed late January 3rd and persisted through the morning hours of January 6th. The heaviest snow accumulations fell across northwest Saint Joseph County where amounts neared 1 foot. There was a report of 11.4 inches in South Bend. Lesser amounts and impacts were experienced across the eastern half of the county. Visibilities reduced to less than a quarter of a mile at times, and snow covered roads, created difficult travel and numerous accidents across the region. There were also road closures reported in Saint Joseph County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front moved rapidly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th. Rain changed to a brief period of freezing rain, then to sleet during the overnight hours. The sleet changed to snow on the 12th as the Arctic air flooded in. A vigorous disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere prolonged the snow through the early evening of the 12th. Total accumulations of sleet were from one-half to one inch in most areas. On top of the sleet, snow accumulated from 3 to 4 inches in most places. Freezing rain produced a thin layer of glaze under the sleet and snow. The highest sleet and snow accumulation was 6 inches at Rockport in Spencer County. Following the changeover to snow, visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile at times. Peak snowfall rates were near one-half inch per hour. Strong north winds frequently gusted to 35 mph, producing wind chills near zero at times. Very cold temperatures in the wake of the storm reduced the effectiveness of melting agents such as salt. Dangerous travel conditions persisted well after the ending of the precipitation. Strong gusty winds caused isolated power outages, including an outage affecting 500 customers in the Boonville area. Authorities reported several vehicle accidents during the morning commute, despite very light traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure over the central Plains brought bitterly cold temperatures and brisk northwest winds to start the new year. Wind chill indices from minus 10 to minus 15 were observed across southwest Indiana during the early and mid-morning hours of the New Year's Day holiday. Actual air temperatures fell to around zero on New Year's morning, including a low of one above zero at the Evansville airport. The Evansville water and sewer utility reported over a dozen water main breaks during the arctic outbreak. One of the water main breaks spilled water onto the Lloyd Expressway. The resulting icy conditions were responsible for an accident involving a truck, which slid into the side of a commecial building. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy lake effect snow developed late January 3rd and persisted through the morning hours of January 6th. The heaviest snow accumulations fell across northern and eastern portions of La Porte County, with totals generally between 10 and 25 inches. There was a report of 25.1 inches of total snow accumulation 1 mile southwest La Porte. Visibilities reduced to less than a quarter of a mile at times, and snow covered roads, created difficult travel and numerous accidents across the region. There were also road closures reported in La Porte County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds gusted to between 40 and 45 mph in the afternoon. The highest reported gust at the Evansville airport was 43 mph. A deep surface low pressure center moved east-northeast from the Kansas City area to northern Illinois. The strong winds occurred ahead of a surface occluded front that arced southeast from the low. The presence of a mid-level dry slot allowed sufficient solar warming for the stronger winds to mix down to the surface in the late afternoon hours.

Storm total snowfall amounts included 8.1 inches 0.6 miles south of Porter; 8.0 inches 2.8 miles east of Portage; 8.0 inches in Burdick; 7.0 inches 4.2 miles east of Chesterton and 6.0 inches in Valparaiso.

A sharp cold front and a deep low pressure system brought widespread precipitation to southern Indiana January 12. As temperatures dropped from the 40s and 50s into the 30s and 20s, rain changed over to freezing rain, sleet, and snow. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 5 inches, which fell on top a glaze to 1/4 inch of ice. Travel was severely impacted with widespread school and business closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of snow fell on top of 0.1 to 0.2 inches of ice accumulation across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure center tracked northeast into the region. Warm air aloft and cold air at the surface combined with the low to create a significant freezing rain event over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed throughout the county due to the ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure center tracked northeast into the region. Warm air aloft and cold air at the surface combined with the low to create a significant freezing rain event over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, significant ice accumulation occurred over a large portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure center tracked northeast into the region. Warm air aloft and cold air at the surface combined with the low to create a significant freezing rain event over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three tenths of an inch of ice was reported. Trees and power lines were down across the county, with Batesville hit particularly hard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and pockets of freezing rain developed during the early morning hours of January 24th across portions of northwest Indiana which led to icy conditions on roadways.  Some light snow also occurred.  Several accidents and spins out were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed along an Arctic cold front as it sliced southeast across southwest Indiana during the morning hours. As the front slowed down over the Tennessee Valley late in the day, a weak surface low developed on the front. This caused snow to linger across Spencer County through the evening hours. The result was an area of heavy snowfall in Spencer County, where 4 inches was reported. Across the remainder of southwest Indiana, including Evansville and Princeton, amounts ranged from 2 to 3 inches. Much of the snow melted on treated road surfaces during the day, but conditions rapidly deteriorated after dark as temperatures dropped through the teens. Wind chill indices were in the teens during most of the snowstorm. Cold temperatures reduced the effectiveness of road salt, which slowed recovery efforts in the wake of the storm. Schools remained closed following the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain between the 20th and 24th sent rivers above flood stage. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. This was greater than 200 percent of normal. This was the wettest February on record at Evansville, with a total of 9.24 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding developed along the Wabash River in the East Mt. Carmel area. Extensive flooding of low-lying agricultural land occurred. Several local roads were flooded, some by several feet of water. The river continued rising into March.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding developed on the Patoka River. Flooding affected primarily farmland. Seasonal camping sites closest to the river became inundated. The river continued rising into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain between the 20th and 24th sent rivers above flood stage. This was the wettest February on record at Evansville, with a total of 9.24 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding developed on the White River. People in residential cabins on the riverward side of the levee relocated. Many local roads were completely impassable. Oil fields were inaccessible. Water pumps were installed on the west end of Hazleton, but some street flooding occurred. The river continued rising into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist 500 mb shortwave trough tracked east from the Plains, causing a surface low pressure center to develop northeast from Texas to the central Appalachians. Widespread wintry precipitation occurred as colder air filtered south on the back side of the low. From one-tenth to two-tenths of an inch of ice glazed surfaces across southwest Indiana. Near the onset of precipitation, a period of sleet was reported. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain. The automated observing station at the Evansville airport measured 0.18 inch of ice accretion. Most of the icing was on trees, power lines, and elevated surfaces during the precipitation. After the precipitation ended, temperatures fell through the 20's, causing icy spots to develop even on paved surfaces. Slick roads and motor vehicle accidents were common during the early morning hours. An ambulance was involved in a nine-vehicle crash that occurred in Vanderburgh County. Another crash involved an overturned snow plow. Area schools were closed.

A winter storm produced heavy snow across extreme portions of southern Indiana March 11 and 12. The highest amounts were across southern Perry, southern Crawford, and Hancock counties were 4 to locally 6 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 6 inches of snow accumulated across Perry County. 5 inches was reported in Tell City and dispatch indicated that several trees and power lines were down due to the heavy wet snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system crossed through the Ohio Valley March 20-21 and led to a band of heavy wet snow over portions of southern Indiana. The highest totals observed were 8-10 inches, with widespread amounts of 4 to 8 inches. The heavy snow led to scattered tree limbs down and power outages. Many schools and businesses were closed, and hazardous travel conditions developed which resulted in traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 4 to 5 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain sent the Ohio River above flood stage. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. This was greater than 200 percent of normal. This was the wettest February on record at Evansville, with a total of 9.24 inches. The river crested at the highest levels since the March, 1997 flood from Newburgh upriver to Spencer County. From Evansville downriver past the mouth of the Wabash River, the Ohio River continued rising through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Ohio River. The flood crest in neighboring Warrick County was the highest since the March, 1997 flood. Numerous roads were closed for days at a time, including at least 50 county roads. U.S. Route 231 north of Rockport was affected by high water, along with a few other state highways. The town of Grandview in Spencer County was hardest hit, where some residents evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very localized flash flooding and isolated minor wind damage occurred across southwest Indiana. A surface low pressure center tracked rapidly north-northeast from Arkansas to the upper Mississippi Valley, dragging a warm front north across southwest Indiana. Instability was adequate for thunderstorms. A very moist air mass resulted in excessive rainfall rates, which produced flash flooding due to the water-logged ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small trees and limbs were blown down in town. A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to at least 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very localized flash flooding and isolated minor wind damage occurred across southwest Indiana. A surface low pressure center tracked rapidly north-northeast from Arkansas to the upper Mississippi Valley, dragging a warm front north across southwest Indiana. Instability was adequate for thunderstorms. A very moist air mass resulted in excessive rainfall rates, which produced flash flooding due to the water-logged ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high water rescue was conducted due to a vehicle stuck in floodwaters on the north side of the Evansville metro area. Heavy rain caused a sinkhole to open up in the city of Evansville near the downtown area. Widespread rainfall rates around one-half inch per hour occurred on saturated ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After widespread heavy rainfall and flooding during the middle and later portions of February, many rivers remained swollen and above flood stage through the first part of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Tell City continued to be above flood stage. The river crested in late February but didn't drop below flood stage until March 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After widespread heavy rainfall and flooding during the middle and later portions of February, many rivers remained swollen and above flood stage through the first part of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Cannelton remained above flood stage during early March. The river crested in late February but didn't drop below flood stage until March 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After widespread heavy rainfall and flooding during the middle and later portions of February, many rivers remained swollen and above flood stage through the first part of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Clifty Creek crested in late February but didn't drop below flood stage until March 1.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Ohio River. At the river gage at Newburgh, the river crested at 50.12 feet on the 28th. Flood stage there is 38 feet. This was the highest flood crest at Newburgh since the flood of March, 1997. Just a few families were forced from their homes. Numerous roads were closed for days at a time, mostly county roads. The banks along Newburgh's scenic riverfront were damaged. Indiana Route 662 and Water Street in Newburgh were affected by high water. In eastern Warrick County, backwater from the Ohio River flooded Caney and Little Pigeon Creeks, flooding a few houses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River rose above flood stage around mid-month and continued rising through the end of the month. Numerous county roads near the river were flooded. An extensive acreage of farmland was inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Wabash River from late February. At the Mount Carmel river gage, the river crested at 29.68 feet on the 3rd of March. Flood stage is 19 feet. Extensive lowland flooding occurred in western Gibson County, with some evacuations needed. Numerous local river roads were flooded. The river was three miles wide at I-64. Cattle and farm equipment were relocated. Access to local campsites was blocked. There was minimal impact to commercial activities because of floodgate closures. Flood waters affected residents of East Mt. Carmel, Indiana. Fifth Street that parallels the river was closed. A restaurant along the river was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major river flooding along the Kankakee River that developed from heavy rainfall in late February 2018 continued to slowly recede during March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along the Kankakee River for most of March with the river finally falling below moderate flood stage on March 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major river flooding along the Kankakee River that developed from heavy rainfall in late February 2018 continued to slowly recede during March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along the Kankakee River during early March with the river finally falling below moderate flood stage on March 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across far northwest Indiana starting during the evening of February 8th and then continuing through the evening of February 9th.  Storm total snowfall amounts included 12.0 inches 4.8 miles east northeast of Gary; 10.3 inches 0.6 miles south of Porter; 9.9 inches 1.5 miles north northwest of Munster; 9.3 inches 1 mile west northwest of Dyer; 9.3 inches 1.1 miles north of Crown Point and 8.0 inches 5.5 miles south southwest of Valparaiso.
Very heavy rain fell across northwest Indiana on February 19th and February 20th which caused areal flooding across some areas and major flooding on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred along the Kankakee River.  The river crested at 13.3 feet at Shelby on February 23rd, which was the highest crest on record.  Flood stage at Shelby is 9 feet and major stage is 12.5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across northwest Indiana on February 19th and February 20th which caused areal flooding across some areas and major flooding on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred along the Kankakee River.  The river crested at 13.65 feet at Dunns Bridge on February 26th, which was the highest crest on record.  Flood stage at Dunns Bridge is 10 feet and major stage is 13 feet.  The river crested at 14.46 feet near Kouts on February 26th, which was the second highest crest on record.  Flood stage near Kouts is 11 feet and major stage is 14 feet.  The flooding damaged culverts and levees near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across northwest Indiana on February 19th and February 20th which caused areal flooding across some areas and major flooding on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred along the Iroquois River.  The river crested at 17.05 feet at Rensselaer on February 23rd, which was the highest crest on record.  Flood stage at Rensselaer is 12 feet and major stage is 15 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Patoka River from late February. At the river gage near Princeton, the river crested at 22.79 feet on March 2. Flooding affected primarily farmland. Many county roads were closed in the vicinity of the river. Seasonal camping trailers closest to the river were flooded or moved to higher ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the White River from late February. People in residential cabins on the riverward side of the levee relocated. Many local roads were completely impassable. Oil fields were inaccessible. Water pumps were installed on the west end of Hazleton, but some street flooding occurred. At the Hazleton river gage, the river crested at 26.30 feet on March 3. Flood stage there is 16 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow release of a snow pack, containing one to over two inches of water, occurred in the days leading to the event which started the process of river rises in many areas. Low pressure tracked from northwest Iowa into northern Lower Michigan, which ushered in a rapid warmup and equally rapid release of any remaining water in the snow-pack. Several rounds of rain occurred between the 19th and 21st of February across the region, fed by record high precipitable water levels (by February standards) in the 1.3 to 1.5 inch range. A swath of four to six inches of rain fell northwest of a Logansport to Kendallville line. All these factors combined to cause extensive flooding on several rivers in the St Joseph, Kankakee and Upper Wabash river basins, with record crests occurring on some rivers. These water levels forced evacuations of homes and closure of businesses and schools, rescues from those driving into flood waters and an overwhelming of water treatment facilities in a few communities. While exact damage figures were not available at the time of entry of the data, preliminary data suggests values into the millions (possibly $10 million or higher).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding occurred across the county as rivers, creeks, streams and ditches could not keep up with the flood waters.  Water was reported to be so high and fast that many ditches were breaking, allowing the water to spill onto roads and other areas. A levee on the Yellow River west of State Route 39 and south of State Route 8 was breached, causing extensive damage to the levee and increased flooding downstream. Portions of State Routes 8, 39, 10 and 23 as well as countless county roads were closed due to high water. Damage is estimated in excess of 1.2 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across northwest Indiana on February 19th and February 20th which caused areal flooding across some areas and major flooding on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred along the Iroquois River.  The river crested at 24.35 feet at Foresman on February 22nd, which was the third highest crest on record.  Flood stage at Foresman is 18 feet and major stage is 24 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the White River from late February. At the Petersburg river gage, the river crested at 25.2 feet on March 2. Flood stage there is 16 feet. A flood has exceeded this level each year since January, 2004. Evacuations began from river cabins. Livestock were moved. Water reached State Route 257. Extensive flooding of low-lying areas inundated numerous Pike County roads, some by several feet of water. These roads included 750N, 600N, 1000E, 250W, 400W, 675N, 700N, 1000W, 900W, 700W, and 775W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Wabash River from late February. At the New Harmony river gage, the river crested at 21.21 feet on March 4th. Flood stage there is 15 feet. Floodwaters encroached on parts of northern New Harmony, requiring sandbagging in town. Harmonie State Park was partially inundated and closed. Some stripper oil wells were flooded along the river from New Harmony south. The river was three miles wide at Interstate 64. Considerable flooding of bottomland agricultural land occurred. Some rural county roads were impacted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Ohio River from late February. At the Mt. Vernon river gage, the river crested at 49 feet on the 4th. Flood stage is 35 feet. This crest was exactly one foot below the flood of March, 1997. There was considerable flooding of low-lying farmland. County roads were underwater. The riverfront park at Mount Vernon was inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued along the Ohio River from late February. The flood crest in neighboring Warrick County was the highest since the March, 1997 flood. Numerous roads were closed for days at a time, including at least 50 county roads. U.S. Route 231 north of Rockport was affected by high water, along with a few other state highways. The town of Grandview in Spencer County was hardest hit, where some residents evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued on the Ohio River from late February. Water from the Ohio River backed up into Pigeon Creek, which flows through Evansville. Some homes were evacuated along Pigeon Creek. Numerous roads were closed for days at a time, mostly county roads in bottomland fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area rivers remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The rivers fell below flood stage by mid-March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued along the Ohio River from late February. After cresting in late February at the highest level since March of 1997, the river fell below flood stage by mid-month. A few families were forced from their homes. Numerous roads were closed for days at a time, mostly county roads. Floodwaters crept over the riverbanks along Newburgh's scenic riverfront. Indiana Route 662 and Water Street in Newburgh were affected by high water. In eastern Warrick County, backwater from the Ohio River flooded Caney and Little Pigeon Creeks, flooding a few houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across southern Indiana from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the southern Indiana counties including road closures, road washouts and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 37 in English at SR 64 under water. Several other streets in Marengo under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, slow-moving low in the upper levels of the atmosphere caused snow to linger across southwest Indiana through much of the night. The upper-level low drifted east across the Ohio Valley. Snowfall amounts were generally from 1 to 2 inches. Most of the snow accumulated on grassy areas. Paved surfaces were generally warm enough to melt the snow, however some icy spots developed as temperatures fell below freezing early on the 21st.

The high water was the result of ditches, creeks, streams and rivers exceeding their capacity, causing extensive flooding that slowly increased over the span of a few days. State Route 8, between US 421 and State Route 39 suffered such severe flooding as a result of flood waters on the Yellow iver that a portion of the road will require complete reconstruction of a portion, causing it to remain closed for several months. See separate entry for damage to a levee in Starke county. Damage in excess of $500,000 was reported to mainly county roads, state highways and the water treatment plant in LaPorte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper-level low moved southeast across the mid Mississippi Valley to the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. This induced the formation of a surface low pressure system over the southeastern states. A small band of heavy snow formed across the lower Ohio Valley as colder air began to funnel into the area during the afternoon hours, changing the rain to snow rather quickly from northwest to southeast. Due to the warm ground and air temperatures at or above freezing, the snow had a hard time sticking on roads until it came down steadily. Visibility was reduced to one-half mile at times. Amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common across southwest Indiana. There was even an area of 4 to 5 inches in Posey County, west of Evansville. The heavy wet snow and gusty winds even downed some tree limbs where the heaviest totals occurred in the Ohio River counties. Roads were in poor condition. Specific snowfall totals included: 4 inches about four miles south of New Harmony (Posey County), 3 inches at Boonville (Warrick County) and Santa Claus (Spencer County), 2 inches at Evansville, and 1 inch at Francisco (Gibson County).

Low pressure tracked from northwest Iowa into northern Lower Michigan, which ushered in a rapid warmup and equally rapid release of any remaining water in the snow-pack. Several rounds of rain occurred between the 19th and 21st of February across the region, fed by record high precipitable water levels (by February standards) in the 1.3 to 1.5 inch range. A swath of four to six inches of rain fell northwest of a Logansport to Kendallville line. All these factors combined to cause extensive flooding on several rivers in the St Joseph, Kankakee and Upper Wabash river basins, with record crests occurring on some rivers. These water levels forced evacuations of homes and closure of businesses and schools, rescues from those driving into flood waters and an overwhelming of water treatment facilities in a few communities. While exact damage figures were not available at the time of entry of the data, preliminary data suggests values into the millions (possibly $10 million or higher).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rainfall in the Tippecanoe River basin forced dam flows in excess of 20,000 CFS. This caused extensive flooding downstream of the dam with some neighborhoods having to have assistance evacuating and some water rescues due to rapid rises. The Diamond Point area near Monticello was impacted by the flood waters. Damage is estimated in excess of 230,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper-level low moved southeast across the mid Mississippi Valley to the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. This induced the formation of a surface low pressure system over the southeastern states. A small band of heavy snow formed across the lower Ohio Valley as colder air began to funnel into the area during the afternoon hours, changing the rain to snow rather quickly from northwest to southeast. Due to the warm ground and air temperatures at or above freezing, the snow had a hard time sticking on roads until it came down steadily. Visibility was reduced to one-half mile at times. Amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common across southwest Indiana. There was even an area of 4 to 5 inches in Posey County, west of Evansville. The heavy wet snow and gusty winds even downed some tree limbs where the heaviest totals occurred in the Ohio River counties. Roads were in poor condition. Specific snowfall totals included: 4 inches about four miles south of New Harmony (Posey County), 3 inches at Boonville (Warrick County) and Santa Claus (Spencer County), 2 inches at Evansville, and 1 inch at Francisco (Gibson County).

A slow-moving cold front drifted slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley on the 27th, then stalled over the Tennessee Valley on the 28th. The front returned north as a warm front and stalled over the Ohio Valley on the 29th. Widespread heavy rainfall from this oscillating front eventually brought rivers above flood stage. The rivers continued rising into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River rose above flood stage on the 29th and continued rising through the end of the month. The flooding during late March was minor, consisting of bottomland woods and fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across northwest Indiana on February 19th and February 20th which caused areal flooding across some areas and major flooding on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many areas of Jasper County were flooded with some roads flooded and closed.  Storm total rainfall amounts included 6.50 inches two miles south southeast of Wheatfield; 6.41 inches one mile north northwest of De Motte and 4.70 inches one mile north of Rensselaer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across northwest Indiana on February 19th and February 20th which caused areal flooding across some areas and major flooding on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred in Porter County which caused flood damage to approximately 190 homes.  Flooding closed several roads and damaged public infrastructure, including roads, culverts, bridges, levees and stream and ditch banks.  Storm total rainfall amounts included 4.50 inches in Portage and 4.20 inches in Valparaiso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low interacted with a surface low in southeast Ohio. A band of heavy snowfall set up northwest of this stacked system, producing 2 to 5 inches of snow along and west of the I-75 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county sheriff's office measured 4 inches of snow in Boston, and 3.5 inches of snow near Richmond.

The strong gusty winds were associated with a surface low pressure system that tracked east-southeast from Iowa to the upper Ohio Valley during the afternoon.

For the second consecutive morning, low temperatures fell into the upper 20's. The back-to-back freezes adversely impacted fruit crops and some flowering plants and shrubs. The high pressure that brought the cold weather was centered over the middle and upper Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. The overall impact on fruits and grains was generally light.

The Ohio River once again poked above flood stage late in the month. This round of flooding was very minor compared to flooding earlier in the month. Low-lying fields and woods were inundated. The planting of cropland was further delayed.

A low pressure system generated numerous thunderstorms across central Indiana on April 3rd. The first few rounds of thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across the area, while the final round of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to flowing water across northern Vigo County from heavy thunderstorm rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system generated numerous thunderstorms across central Indiana on April 3rd. The first few rounds of thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across the area, while the final round of thunderstorms brought damaging winds and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-0 tornado, with a max wind speed of 85 mph, affected one homestead and a thin band of trees. Damage at the homestead included barn skylights being blown out from wind entering an east-facing opening, debris from half an outbuilding`s roof and collapsed wall sent into an adjacent field to the northeast, downed tree branches with one breaking the garage window, and the top horizontal portion of a power pole being broken off. Also, straight-line wind damage was noted south and east of Buck Creek including downed trees and an outbuilding shifted off its foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought snow to central Indiana late on April 1. Some areas received around 6 inches of snow. The 2.1 inches of snow at Indianapolis set a record for the date. The snow was also the most ever recorded on an Easter Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of a six inch snowfall came in from two different locations within Clinton County, Rossville and 4 miles north-northeast of Frankfort. No other impacts were reported.

The DNR said his body was found about 100 yards away from his kayak, located between Tannehill Bridge and Lowell Bridge on the Driftwood River.||It is believed the 26-year-old man drowned while kayaking on suspected flood waters of the Driftwood River near Columbus on the morning of April 5th. Moderate flooding was occurring on the East Fork White River at Columbus which is slightly downstream from the incident. The time and location of the report is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River for much of April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued from March because the moist ground was primed for more flooding. The main effect of the flooding was to delay the planting of crops in low-lying agricultural land near the river. Corn planting is normally done by mid May. Some rural roads were closed by backwater flooding of tributary creeks such as Pigeon Creek. Basements along the Evansville riverfront began to fill.

Moderate flooding continued on the Patoka River from late March. At the river gage near Princeton, the river crested at 20.70 feet on April 5. Flood stage is 18 feet. Flooding affected primarily farmland. Some rural county roads were closed in the vicinity of the river. Seasonal camping trailers closest to the river were moved to higher ground.

The White River remained above flood stage from late March through mid April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued from March because the moist ground was primed for more flooding. The main effect of the flooding was to delay the planting of crops in low-lying agricultural land near the river. Corn planting is normally done by mid May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the White River from late March. People in residential cabins on the riverward side of the levee relocated. Many local roads were completely impassable. Oil fields were inaccessible. Water pumps were installed on the west end of Hazleton, but some street flooding occurred. This flood crest was about one-half foot higher than the flood a month earlier, in March. At the Hazleton river gage, the river crested at 26.80 feet on April 10. Flood stage there is 16 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The White River remained above flood stage from late March through mid April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued from March because the moist ground was primed for more flooding. The main effect of the flooding was to delay the planting of crops in low-lying agricultural land near the river. Corn planting is normally done by mid May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the White River from late March. At the Petersburg river gage, the river crested at 25.49 feet on April 9. Flood stage there is 16 feet. A flood has exceeded the 25-foot level each year since January, 2004. This flood crest was just a few inches higher than the crest a month earlier, in March. Evacuations began from river cabins. Livestock were moved. Water reached State Route 257. Extensive flooding of low-lying areas inundated numerous Pike County roads, some by several feet of water. These roads included 750N, 600N, 1000E, 250W, 400W, 675N, 700N, 1000W, 900W, 700W, and 775W.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was reported down.

In addition to wind damage, brief tropical rainfall rates caused minor water problems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare April snowfall was observed in southwest Indiana, mainly south of the Interstate 64 corridor. As surface low pressure moved from east Texas into Mississippi and Alabama, most precipitation on the afternoon and evening of April 6th stayed to the south. However, a secondary low developed and interacted with enough leftover moisture to produce a band of precipitation overnight into the morning hours on April 7th. Cold air funneled into the region through the night, allowing precipitation to quickly change over to snow, with a very brief transition period of sleet for some locations. Many locations picked up anywhere from 0.5 to 1 of snow, particularly along the Ohio River. Impacts to travel were minimal due to warm pavement temperatures, which melted the snow on roadways. The highest reported snow amount was 1 inch in Newburgh in Warrick County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of nickels. As a low pressure center developed over the western Plains, a warm front lifted north across southwest Indiana. An isolated strong thunderstorm tracked east along the front, producing hail along its path across Evansville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny-size hail occurred near Skylane Airport. Dime to nickel-size hail fell on the north side of Evansville. Hail covered the ground in some locations. Several vehicles slid off the hail-covered roads.

Gusty south to southwest winds shifted to a more westerly direction as a strong cold front associated with the low pressure system moved across the region. Peak wind gusts were measured up to 47 mph at Evansville. Most other peak wind gusts in southwest Indiana ranged from 35 to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River remained above flood stage from late March through much of April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued because the heavy rainfall in March primed the ground for more flooding. The severity of the flooding was moderate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River remained above flood stage from late March through much of April. Flooding of farmland was extensive, and farmers were forced to delay the planting of crops. Corn is normally planted by mid May. At the Mount Carmel river gage, the river crested at 29.06 feet on the 10th. Flood stage is 19 feet. This flood crest was about half a foot lower then the flood a month earlier, in March. Extensive lowland flooding occurred in western Gibson County, with some evacuations needed. Numerous local river roads were flooded. The river was three miles wide at I-64. Cattle and farm equipment were relocated. Access to local campsites was blocked. There was minimal impact to commercial activities because of floodgate closures. Flood waters affected residents of East Mt. Carmel, Indiana. Fifth Street that parallels the river was closed. A restaurant along the river was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River remained above flood stage from late March through much of April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued because the heavy rainfall in March primed the ground for more flooding. The severity of the flooding was moderate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Wabash River from late March. At the New Harmony river gage, the river crested at 20.68 feet on April 11th. Flood stage there is 15 feet. Flooding of farmland was extensive, and farmers were forced to delay the planting of crops. Corn is normally planted by mid May. Floodwaters encroached on parts of northern New Harmony, requiring sandbagging in town. Harmonie State Park was partially inundated and closed. Some stripper oil wells were flooded along the river from New Harmony south. The river was three miles wide at Interstate 64. Considerable flooding of bottomland agricultural land occurred. Some rural county roads were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River for much of April. Even though rainfall was pretty close to normal in April, flooding continued from March because the moist ground was primed for more flooding. The main effect of the flooding was to delay the planting of crops in low-lying agricultural land near the river. Corn planting is normally done by mid May. Some rural roads were closed by backwater flooding of tributary creeks such as Pigeon Creek. Basements along the Evansville riverfront began to fill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of lines of showers and thunderstorms developed across central Illinois and moved southeast into central Indiana during much of the day into the evening on June 10th. The first line produced some damaging wind and small hail in the northeast. The next batch provided mainly thunderstorm wind gusts across west-central and central Indiana. After that, large hail was reported in Knox County during the late afternoon, with flash flooding reported during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs of unknown size had broken off of a healthy tree due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. This report was relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged period of hazardous heat and humidity that began on June 29th persisted through the first several days of July. The most dangerous heat index values occurred on July 5th, when afternoon heat indices peaked around 110 degrees. Otherwise, the peak heat index each afternoon was near 105. Actual air temperatures were mostly in the lower to mid 90's. South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

Heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding across a portion of Jennings County as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some street flooding, perhaps a few inches deep, was observed on Washington Street near 16th Street in Columbus due to heavy rainfall. This report was received via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of lines of showers and thunderstorms developed across central Illinois and moved southeast into central Indiana during much of the day into the evening on June 10th. The first line produced some damaging wind and small hail in the northeast. The next batch provided mainly thunderstorm wind gusts across west-central and central Indiana. After that, large hail was reported in Knox County during the late afternoon, with flash flooding reported during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel sized hail was observed in this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving, compact MCV moved off of Lake Michigan during the afternoon hours of the 9th. A residual stationary boundary stretched roughly from Michigan City southeastward to Van Wert in the morning of the 9th. As the MCV moved further off Lake Michigan, the boundary lifted northward as a warm front, which eventually became orientated northwest to southeast across southern Steuben/northern DeKalb counties. Thunderstorms developed with isolated tornadoes occurring in Steuben county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to witnesses, a weak, but multi funnel tornado touched down in an empty field, then intensified somewhat as it tracked East Northeast, removing a portion of a roof. It also caused damage to two barn structures as it crossed County Road 150 W.  Maximum winds are estimated at around 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of central Indiana during the afternoon hours of June 1st.  As these storms pushed generally south or south-southeast, a few of them downed trees, limbs, power lines across locations south of Interstate 70.  One of the strong thunderstorms moved across Monroe Lake and a 26-year-old man that was swimming in the lake drowned as the wind and the rain kicked up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed in this location due to strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated storms developed north of a warm front that was draped generally along and south of U.S. 24. While sufficient instability existed, weak shear was in place, keeping the storms more of the pulse variety. A few of the storms produced hail approaching severe levels.

Frontal boundary remained stalled across the region with a moderately unstable atmosphere in place to allow for development of thunderstorms. A few of the storms produced locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a partially rotten tree was blown down onto residence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: WTHR-13 reported that Indiana Conservation officers found the body of 26-year-old man around 7:30pm Sunday the 3rd while searching Monroe Lake with side scan sonar equipment. He was in about 15 feet of water near the area where he had been swimming from his boat.||An Indiana Department of Natural Resources spokesperson said the man was swimming off a pontoon he rented with three friends when he disappeared Friday evening as a thunderstorm with strong winds passed overhead.||Friends say the man was a good swimmer, which might have been why he went into the water without a life jacket. When a storm suddenly kicked up out on the lake though, it was too much for him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged period of hazardous heat and humidity that began on June 29th persisted through the first several days of July. The most dangerous heat index values occurred on July 5th, when afternoon heat indices peaked around 110 degrees. Otherwise, the peak heat index each afternoon was near 105. Actual air temperatures were mostly in the lower to mid 90's. South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the periphery of an 500 mb ridge across portions of northern Indiana and southern Lower Michigan. While overall shear and moisture was limited, some drier air allowed for favorable DCAPE's in the 1000 to 1200 J/KG range. Wind damage was reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported two small sheds were blown over at a local hardware store.

A frontal system moved through central Indiana during the afternoon of July 20th, producing thunderstorms. Some of the storms were severe with damaging winds, large hail, and one EF-1 tornado in Madison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado was observed and recorded via video on Friday afternoon in Moonville in Madison County. Madison County Emergency Management, in coordination with the National Weather Service in Indianapolis, conducted a storm survey and determined a tornado touched down and destroyed a barn, caused roof damage to an adjacent home, produced tree damage, and flipped a storage shed onto its side. The track of the tornado began in a soybean field to the west-southwest of the barn. The maximum winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon interacted with a surface front to produce widespread heavy rain across much of central Indiana. The moisture content of the atmosphere was very high. Precipitable water, a measure of moisture content in the atmosphere, is normally around 1 inch for early September in Indiana. Precipitable water amounts approached 2 inches for parts of the area on September 8th. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 4 inches fell on the 7th of September, while the 72-hour total rainfall amounts ending the morning of September 9th came in between 4 and 6 inches were common with some locally higher amounts. This caused some Flash Flooding in the southern half of the area, and more general flooding at other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported to be over County Road 550, near Marchino Road, due to heavy rainfall. This report was relayed by Knox County EMA.

Heavy rainfall of 1 to 4 inches fell on the 7th of September, while the 72-hour total rainfall amounts ending the morning of September 9th came in between 4 and 6 inches were common with some locally higher amounts. This caused some Flash Flooding in the southern half of the area, and more general flooding at other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads were closed in portions of Clay County with water flowing over them due to heavy rainfall. Portions of County Roads 300 East and 400 East were closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media and law enforcement reported a large rotten tree, roughly 20 years old, was blown over by non severe thunderstorm winds . The tree landed onto a moving truck that was occupied by a man and woman. Both occupants were pronounced deceased at the scene. The base of the tree was rotten in over half way and up 5 feet with the portion of the tree breaking off being the rotten portion. No other damage to structures, trees or any object were noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was photographed by numerous individuals. The funnel cloud occurred in association with weak convection, which was driven mainly by afternoon heating. High pressure at the surface and aloft dominated the region. Low level lapse rates were quite steep, on the order of 7 to 7.5 degrees Celsius per kilometer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous photos were taken of a funnel cloud over the area of U.S. Highway 41 in northern Vanderburgh County. The parent cell was a weak shower or thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms organized into a large cluster over southwest Indiana during the evening. These storms produced a couple of very damaging downbursts. The storms occurred in a very unstable environment ahead of a cold front. This cold front extended from southern Indiana and Illinois into southeast Missouri and northern Arkansas. As the front shifted south, the main 500 mb shortwave over Missouri dove southeastward across the lower Mississippi Valley. Wind shear profiles favored organized storm structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted. Tree limbs were broken. A tree fell on a garage, damaging part of its roof and wall. A tall fence was blown down at the high school tennis court. The peak winds were estimated near 90 mph in this downburst, which was about 1.5 miles long and 0.75 mile wide.

As the front shifted south, the main 500 mb shortwave over Missouri dove southeastward across the lower Mississippi Valley. Wind shear profiles favored organized storm structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very damaging downburst began in extreme southeast Warrick County, along and just north of Indiana Route 66. Tree limbs were blown down in Warrick County. As the storm crossed into Spencer County, wind speeds increased rapidly to near 95 mph. A tree was uprooted along the county line. The vast majority of the eight-mile long downburst occurred in Spencer County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damaging downburst crossed into Spencer County from southeast Warrick County. Hundreds of trees, some very large, were uprooted, snapped, or had large broken limbs. A half dozen homes or garages sustained mostly minor damage, with loss of siding, shingles, or other damage. One barn was destroyed east of Hatfield due to fallen trees. A tree fell on a mobile home. Peak winds in this downburst were estimated near 95 mph. The damage area was about eight miles long and two miles wide, and nearly all of it occurred in Spencer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across southern Indiana from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the southern Indiana counties including road closures, road washouts and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads closed across county due to flash flooding. At its peak, 32 roads were closed across county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon merged with a cold front over the mid-section of the country. The tropical moisture squeezed out by the front produced widespread heavy rainfall and isolated flooding. During the afternoon hours, there was enough sunshine and destabilization for thunderstorms to develop. Enough low-level wind shear was present for a few rotating storms and an isolated weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down in a soybean field. Peak winds were estimated near 65 mph. The only evidence of the tornado was obtained from video footage. Damage assessment teams from the National Weather Service and the Spencer County Emergency Management Agency found no damage.

Several storms developed that produced a variety of hail sizes, including a few reaching the size of golfballs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon merged with a cold front over the mid-section of the country. The tropical moisture squeezed out by the front produced widespread heavy rainfall and isolated flooding. During the afternoon hours, there was enough sunshine and destabilization for thunderstorms to develop. Enough low-level wind shear was present for a few rotating storms and an isolated weak tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and snow melt upstream led to significant rises on area rivers in the middle of the month. Portions of the Ohio River went into flood starting in the middle of the month, remaining above flood stage into March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Cannelton Lock went above Flood Stage (42') on February 19, and exceeded Major Flood Stage (46') from 0330 CST February 23 into March.  The river crested at 50.68' at 2045 CST on February 26, which was the highest level since 1997, and the 7th highest stage on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain and snow melt upstream led to significant rises on area rivers in the middle of the month. Portions of the Ohio River went into flood starting in the middle of the month, remaining above flood stage into March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Tell City went into moderate flood stage. The river crested at 49.3 feet which is 11.30 feet above flood stage. The river remained above flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall across the entire Ohio River basin totaled between 8 to 9 inches across southern Indiana from February 15 to February 28. These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the southern Indiana counties including road closures, road washouts and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Clifty Creek went into moderate flood stage. The river crested at 457.30 feet on February 26 (6.30' above flood stage), which was the highest crest since 1997 and the 13th highest on record for the location. The river remained above flood stage into March.

These totals were generally 7+ inches, or 200 to 400% of normal values for mid to late February. The large areal extent of the excessive rainfall led to significant rises on area rivers, including the Ohio River. This resulted in numerous flash flood reports across all of the southern Indiana counties including road closures, road washouts and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bridge between Bushmans Lake Road and Rivers Edge Marina impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted southwest Indiana on the last day of May. Damaging winds and flooding were observed in this outbreak. The first round of storms moved through southwest Indiana during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Widespread damaging winds were associated with this line of storms. Scattered thunderstorm development then began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. The first round of storms left an outflow boundary across the lower Ohio Valley, where thunderstorms increased during the night. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the nighttime round of storms, which caused some flooding early on the morning of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down, and power outages were reported mainly on the west side of Princeton. Trees were also down in East Mt. Carmel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few small clusters of severe thunderstorms moved southeast, producing swaths of large hail and isolated strong wind gusts. The storms occurred along a warm front that extended from central Illinois southeast into northwest Kentucky. The storms occurred during the warmest and most unstable time of day, when surface-based capes were near 2,000 j/kg.

Ping pong ball size hail was reported on the north side of the city. Quarter-size hail fell on the northeast and west sides of the city. Several windshields were shattered or damaged. Strong winds were associated with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon merged with a cold front over the mid-section of the country. The tropical moisture squeezed out by the front produced widespread heavy rainfall and isolated flooding. During the afternoon hours, there was enough sunshine and destabilization for thunderstorms to develop. Enough low-level wind shear was present for a few rotating storms and an isolated weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded. State Route 64 was flooded. Several county roads were closed. A 24-hour rainfall amount of 6.30 inches was reported in Fort Branch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over State Routes 56 and 64. The co-operative observer in Stendal measured 6.60 inches in a 48-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fronts pushed across the region between the 22nd and 27th at the same time a longwave trough set up over the central Plains. Widespread showers and thunderstorms brought periods of rain. The heaviest rains fell across southern Illinois and southwest Indiana, where the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon produced flooding earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway 66 was flooded west of Rockport. Water was over Highway 245 south of Santa Claus. Numerous county roads were flooded across the county. High water was over County Road 1600N near Santa Claus. Several water rescues were conducted due to stranded vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain and a few embedded thunderstorms moved through the Ohio Valley in association with the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water along Laughery Creek left several roads impassable. State Route 62 was closed due to high water and two cars were trapped in the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon interacted with a surface front to produce widespread heavy rain across much of central Indiana. The moisture content of the atmosphere was very high. Precipitable water, a measure of moisture content in the atmosphere, is normally around 1 inch for early September in Indiana. Precipitable water amounts approached 2 inches for parts of the area on September 8th. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 4 inches fell on the 7th of September, while the 72-hour total rainfall amounts ending the morning of September 9th came in between 4 and 6 inches were common with some locally higher amounts. This caused some flash flooding to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of flash flooding across Muncie due to heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This very small, skipping tornado did its damage primarily on hilltops between Sparks Ferry Road and the intersection of State Road 135 and Lewellen Road. A few tree limbs and a small fence were downed near Sparks Ferry Road before it moved northeast over a large wooded area and open farmland. The next damage was concentrated about a quarter mile south of Water Tower Road, just west of Highway 135. Here the tornado tore multiple metal roof panels off a barn, blew the southward-facing overhead doors of a garage in, and tore the entrance door off the garage on the west side. The attached home sustained considerable siding and roof damage. A small play structure on the west side of the home was destroyed, with the debris blown to the west-northwest. Debris from the barn and home were scattered in a narrow path through row crops up to a third of a mile to the northeast, where a resident witnessed large sheets of roofing and siding fall into a hay field on the north side of Lewellen Road. A corn field on the south side of Lewellen Road showed cyclonic rotation in a narrow path of flattened corn.  The maximum wind speeds with this tornado were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of severe storms hammered southern Indiana and central Kentucky on Friday, July 20, 2018, bringing very large hail, wind damage, heavy rain, and 2 confirmed tornadoes in Indiana.  ||The largest hail report the national weather service received was 4 inches in diameter (softball size) in Tompkinsville! The strongest measured wind gust was 70 mph at the Lexington Bluegrass Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was confirmed of EF1 scale with a maximum wind speed of 105 mph. The tornado first touched down along Justin`s Trail in the Southwind Estates subdivision. There was significant tree damage along Pleasure Ridge Road where a large tree fell on a garage. Farther east along Pleasure Ridge Road, several small funnels from the main funnel did extensive damage. A 2 x 8 impaled a concrete grain silo. At least four big barns received extensive damage, including a turkey barn. Further east, several funnels congealed into one main tornado doing extensive damage to hardwood trees and significant roof damage to several homes. Along South Pleasant Road, one home had mud spatter on the back east side of the house where several pieces of wood were impaled in the ground. At that property, a pole barn was completely destroyed and thrown in different directions. A truck and horse trailer was moved and twisted 50 feet. The wind speeds in this area were the strongest at 105 mph and the maximum width was 250 yards. ||At the end of Sarabeth Way, there was extensive hardwood tree damage which included uprooted, twisted, and mangled trees. This included several healthy hickory trees which were snapped. Further east on Sinker Road, there was impressive cyclonic crop damage across a corn field with corn laying in every direction. On Simler Road, there was consistent snapped tree damage and we observed a camper that had been tossed and flipped over and several barns received extensive damage. During the final stages of the tornado, it struck homes along Peyton Road flipping over single wide trailers, lifting the roof of a barn, and doing extensive tree damage. Parts of the barn, including insulation and sheet metal, were thrown into the trees.||According to a local TV news broadcast, a New Middletown Volunteer Fire Department member suffered a minor injury. ||The tornado was captured on video by a security camera and a resident's cell phone in the Southwind Estates, which were circulated on social media.  These videos showed an extremely small but visible vortex (one to three feet in diameter) on the ground going through a grassy area and splitting a tree in front of a home.  One of the videos continued to show the tornado after this, where it grew in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 8th, the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon was centered in southeast Missouri. Much of the moisture and energy from the system interacted with a stationary front running west to east through Kentucky. This produced efficient rainfall that caused flash flooding, and the environmental shear was strong enough to produce two tornadoes along the Ohio River in southern Indiana and north-central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding was reported in Corydon. Water covered roads that do not normally flood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on Main Street in Tell City, midway between Steuben and Winkeireid Streets, doing minor damage to one roof and bending an amateur radio antenna tower. Moving east-northeast, the tornado damaged roofs and snapped several large tree limbs, as well as toppling a large but rotting tree that crushed the roof of a garage.  A small shed was lofted out of its yard and became entangled in utility lines 30 yards to the northeast.  As it crossed Winkeireid Street between 10th and 11th Streets, it buckled in a large overhead door and caused minor damage to the roof of the Indiana Southern Power building.  Continuing to the northeast, a large metal carport was lifted and blown 50 feet north into neighboring trees, and more tree limbs were snapped, while a vinyl fence was blown northwest, as was a large trampoline and a patio umbrella, which was entangled in utility lines northwest of its origin.||The tornado continued to cause tree damage as it moved east of 14th street into a more rural area. On Brushy Fork Rd, the tornado snapped the trunks of three large trees, then continued northeast into a wooded area. The last evidence of the tornado was at the north end of Quaker Rd, where small branches were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 25th a saturated air mass was in place ahead of an advancing cold front, with precipitable water values near two inches. With this air mass and shear ahead of the cold front, convective showers formed over southern Indiana and advanced east along Interstate 65 causing flooding issues in the system's path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported that water was running across the road at State Road 66 and Indian Ridge Road. It was estimated that rainfall rates were 4 per hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced water over the road in low-lying areas on County Road 350 North and State Road 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate to heavy rainfall moved northeast over portions of central Indiana influenced by a warm front lifting northeast during the afternoon of August the 20th. A more intense portion of the band moved over Carroll County producing a single wind damage report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn roof was torn off, multiple large trees were downed, and a power line was down due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor river flooding occurred as a result of a stretch of wet weather during the early part of the month. Average rainfall amounts between the 5th and 10th were between 3 and 4 inches. Much of this rain was due to the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon, which got caught up with a cold front moving through the central states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River. Several Pike County roads were flooded, including (east of State Route 57) County Roads 750N, 600N, and 1000E. West of State Route 57, flooded county roads included 250W, 400W, 675N and 775W. Flooding affected low agricultural lands and low oil fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of scattered showers and thunderstorms moved southeast across the northern half of central Indiana during the late evening of August 6th and early morning of August the 7th. A few of the stronger storms produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A privacy fence was blown over and a large garden shed was destroyed due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. This report was relayed by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of scattered showers and thunderstorms moved southeast across the northern half of central Indiana during the late evening of August 6th and early morning of August the 7th. A few of the stronger storms produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph thunderstorm wind gust was observed in this location. This report was relayed by social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed on power lines, a transformer exploded, and a pole caught fire due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. This event occurred along Allisonville Road, just south of 56th Street.  The road was closed in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front entered the region, interacting with MLCAPE values above 1000 J/KG, but limited shear. PWAT's were approaching two inches in some areas causing some precipitation loading and subsequent brief downbursts or bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported two large tree limbs snapped, with one falling onto a play set.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fronts pushed across the region between the 22nd and 27th at the same time a longwave trough set up over the central Plains. Widespread showers and thunderstorms brought periods of rain. The heaviest rains fell across southern Illinois and southwest Indiana, where the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon produced flooding earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters were rushing over a street intersection in a residential neighborhood near the University of Southern Indiana. A car was stalled in floodwater on a street on the western side of Evansville. In the city, the eastbound ramp of the Lloyd Expressway to southbound U.S. Highway 41 was closed due to high water. A Cocorahs station six miles west-northwest of Evansville measured a daily total of 2.31 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCV from overnight convection in Michigan as well as a upper level wave all caused favorable conditions for thunderstorms to develop. MLCAPE of 2000 to 3000 J/KG was observed with a 40 knot speed max passing through all resulting in pockets of damaging winds. A tornado was confirmed in the Warsaw Indiana area within a larger swath of more sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a tree split from the strong winds and fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts to 60 mph as well as observed a medium size tree limb was blown down blocking half of State Route 119.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey of damage confirmed a low end EF-1 tornado touched down in Warsaw |just southeast of Pike lake and moved through a subdivision causing mainly tree damage. The tornado crossed US-30 and paralleled County Road 75 N, continuing to  uprooted trees, snapping tree trunks and topping trees. Several homes and cars were impacted by tree debris. In addition several power poles were snapped. Minor |roofing and siding damage occurred to some homes as well. The tornado curved to the south slightly, eventually lifting east of North County Road 450 East. Wind speeds were estimated at around 90 mph. ||The tornado occurred along the northern edge of a broader wind damage|swath of up to 250 yards. Damage here was much more sporadic with|estimated winds of 60-65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms moved across parts of central Indiana on the late afternoon and early evening of August 25, bringing damaging winds and hail. Some areas saw baseball size hail while others saw winds of 100 mph. Minor wind damage was reported elsewhere across central Indiana, but the most significant damage occurred in Rush County. Winds of 100 mph caused extensive crop damage, tossed a camper to a home, and downed some trees.
