A short bowing line of thunderstorms moved east across southwest Indiana, causing isolated wind damage to trees. The storms occurred ahead of a warm front that extended southeast from a low pressure center over northwest Missouri. The storms occurred along a corridor of stronger low level winds, averaging near 30 knots at 1 km agl. Steep low level lapse rates allowed strong winds to reach the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down. Power lines were also down.

Some areas saw baseball size hail while others saw winds of 100 mph. Minor wind damage was reported elsewhere across central Indiana, but the most significant damage occurred in Rush County. Winds of 100 mph caused extensive crop damage, tossed a camper to a home, and downed some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a house due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development with upper level support aiding in 30 to 40 knots of shear. Both supercells and bowing segments developed. Isolated damage was reported with spotters monitoring well defined wall cloud and funnel cloud in Whitley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported lightning struck a barn, causing a fire. The fire killed 2 goats and 3 horses. Owners of the animals attempted to rescue them with 2 adult males suffering minor burns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance, abundant moisture and localized shear allowed low topped showers to develop across White and Cass counties. One of the showers produced a brief, weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drone survey indicated that numerous crops were pushed down in a convergent path in a field south of County Road 1250 South. A residence on the north side of the road suffered minor shingle damage with a nearby barn losing part of its roof and a tree uprooted in the yard. The tornado quickly lifted after impact the residence. Maximum winds of 60 to 65 mph are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 10 year old boy was found face down in Lake Michigan about 40 yards from shore near the Indiana Dunes State Park north of Chesterton. Rescuers performed CPR and he was rushed to a nearby hospital where he was pronounced dead. About an hour later, a 14 year old boy was rescued by two police officers about 50 yards from shore. This boy was rushed to the same nearby hospital then airlifted to a children's hospital where he later passed away. Waves were estimated around 4 feet.

A MCV from overnight convection in Michigan as well as a upper level wave all caused favorable conditions for thunderstorms to develop. MLCAPE of 2000 to 3000 J/KG was observed with a 40 knot speed max passing through all resulting in pockets of damaging winds. A tornado was confirmed in the Warsaw Indiana area within a larger swath of more sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported several tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted southwest Indiana on the last day of May.  The first round of storms moved through southwest Indiana during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Scattered thunderstorm development then began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the nighttime round of storms, which caused some flooding early on the morning of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple vehicle water rescues were conducted. Numerous roads were covered by water, including Highway 70, Highway 62 near 75W, County Road 1400N, and County Road 1600N just east of Santa Claus. Some flooded roads were closed through the morning hours. Radar rainfall estimates were from 3 to 4.5 inches across much of Spencer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent, slow-moving thunderstorms dumped very heavy rain during the night of the 11th through mid-morning on the 12th. Including the storms on the 10th, the rainfall at Hazleton was measured at just over 10 inches by a former NWS co-op observer there. The storms on the night of the 11th were aided by a weak upper level disturbance that tracked from south central Illinois across southwest Indiana. A broad low level inflow of unstable air from the west-southwest enhanced the heavy rainfall. Elevated instability up to 2000 j/kg was transported into southwest Indiana by 850 mb winds around 20 to 25 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge over Robb Creek in Hazleton was washed out by high water. The bridge was described as an old I-beam bridge. The bridge was located on a lightly traveled side street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms over Illinois gradually evolved into a bowing line of storms as it approached southwest Indiana. Pockets of damaging winds and a 25-mile long microburst accompanied the line of storms across southwest Indiana. The storms increased in intensity as a result of strong heating and destabilization during the late morning hours. Localized areas of training and repeat cell development occurred in the vicinity of a surface warm front ahead of the complex, contributing to isolated flash flooding. This round of storms was followed by another round of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst produced a long swath of damaging winds across far northern Posey County. The swath was as much as two miles wide in places. Several corn fields were blown down, with the stalks broken in some areas. Hundreds of trees were broken or uprooted, and several power lines were down. Widespread tree damage occurred along Interstate 64 near the Black River rest area. Some small sheds and signs were damaged. The 25-mile long microburst continued across southern Gibson County, where it became more intense and damaging.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heat indices peaked between 100 and 105 degrees on three consecutive days from the 16th to the 18th. Actual air temperatures reached the lower to mid 90's. When these temperatures were combined with dew points in the mid 70's, the heat index surpassed the century mark. A strong 500 mb high was centered over the lower Ohio and middle Mississippi Valleys during this heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms over Illinois gradually evolved into a bowing line of storms as it approached southwest Indiana. The storms increased in intensity as a result of strong heating and destabilization during the late morning hours. Localized areas of training and repeat cell development occurred in the vicinity of a surface warm front ahead of the complex, contributing to isolated flash flooding. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst produced a long swath of damaging winds through much of southern Gibson County. The swath was as much as two miles wide in places. The damage was most intense and frequent in and near Fort Branch, where at least ten homes sustained mainly minor damage, with loss of shingles and other exterior covering. At least two barns were heavily damaged in the Fort Branch area. Several corn fields were blown down, with the stalks broken in some areas. Hundreds of trees were broken or uprooted, and several power lines were down. Numerous small sheds, signs, and other outbuildings were damaged. A semi truck was blown over on the south side of Fort Branch. The 25-mile long microburst began in northwest Posey County, then moved  across southern Gibson County, where it became more intense and damaging.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in east central Missouri during the afternoon along a remnant outflow boundary from the morning thunderstorms. The afternoon storms moved east-southeast along the slowing outflow boundary. The storms intensified in the unstable air near the boundary. Afternoon heating contributed to moderately strong instability. Rather strong wind shear resulted in a bowing line of storms that produced isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in Evansville. A tree fell on a house on the west side of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was draped along and south of U.S.-24 in northern Indiana. Pulse thunderstorms developed in a unstable but poorly sheared environment. One storm produced marginally severe hail.

Southward sagging stationary frontal zone augmented with outflow particularly from intense West to East cluster of intense storms that developed mid afternoon across southern WI eastward through southwestern Lower Michigan. High precipitable water values, very unstable conditions and DCAPE around 1000 J/KG were in place with a poorly sheared environment. Several small bowing segments developed in areas causing localized swaths of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported several trees down across the city of Huntington. At least three cars and a motorcycle suffered damage from falling trees or tree limbs. No injuries were reported.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon interacted with a surface front to produce widespread heavy rain across much of central Indiana. The moisture content of the atmosphere was very high. Precipitable water, a measure of moisture content in the atmosphere, is normally around 1 inch for early September in Indiana. Precipitable water amounts approached 2 inches for parts of the area on September 8th. Heavy rainfall of 1 to 4 inches fell on the 7th of September, while the 72-hour total rainfall amounts ending the morning of September 9th came in between 4 and 6 inches were common with some locally higher amounts. This caused some Flash Flooding in the southern half of the area, and more general flooding at other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported high water over portions of State Road 550 between Bruceville and Wheatland.

A strong 500 mb high moved slowly east from the Mississippi Valley to the Appalachian Mountains during this heat wave.
Heavy rain caused flooding in parts of northwest Indiana during the afternoon of June 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.25 inches was measured in 60 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent, slow-moving thunderstorms dumped very heavy rain during the night of the 11th through mid-morning on the 12th. Including the storms on the 10th, the rainfall at Hazleton was measured at just over 10 inches by a former NWS co-op observer there. The storms on the night of the 11th were aided by a weak upper level disturbance that tracked from south central Illinois across southwest Indiana. A broad low level inflow of unstable air from the west-southwest enhanced the heavy rainfall. Elevated instability up to 2000 j/kg was transported into southwest Indiana by 850 mb winds around 20 to 25 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was reported on several county roads. Low-lying areas around the county were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms organized over southeast Illinois and then moved southeast across the lower Wabash Valley to the White River. The storms encountered increased southeasterly winds along a warm front draped from east central Illinois southward into far southwest IN. The enhanced inflow of very moist air into the storms aided heavy rain and flooding potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was over numerous roads, especially in and near Hazleton and Oakland City. An automated rain gage reporting site near Oakland City measured 2.62 inches in 6 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms impacted southwest Indiana on the last day of May.  The first round of storms moved through southwest Indiana during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Scattered thunderstorm development then began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the nighttime round of storms, which caused some flooding early on the morning of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sections of Highway 168 near Owensville were water-covered. Other nearby roads in rural Gibson County were water-covered as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure moved southeast from the northern Plains into the lower Ohio Valley. Clear skies and light winds set the stage for widespread frost and a brief period of sub-freezing temperatures. Low temperatures ranged from the mid 20's along Interstate 64 to near 30 along the Ohio River. The growing season was ended by this first freeze of the season.

Several rounds of heavy rain between the 20th and 24th sent rivers above flood stage. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. This was greater than 200 percent of normal. This was the wettest February on record at Evansville, with a total of 9.24 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding developed along the Wabash River. Floodwaters encroached on parts of northern New Harmony, requiring sandbagging in town. Harmonie State Park was partially inundated and closed. Some stripper oil wells were flooded along the river from New Harmony south. The river was three miles wide at Interstate 64. Considerable flooding of bottomland agricultural land occurred. Some rural county roads were impacted. The river continued rising into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow release of a snow pack, containing one to over two inches of water, occurred in the days leading to the event which started the process of river rises in many areas. Low pressure tracked from northwest Iowa into northern Lower Michigan, which ushered in a rapid warmup and equally rapid release of any remaining water in the snow-pack. Several rounds of rain occurred between the 19th and 21st of February across the region, fed by record high precipitable water levels (by February standards) in the 1.3 to 1.5 inch range. A swath of four to six inches of rain fell northwest of a Logansport to Kendallville line. All these factors combined to cause extensive flooding on several rivers in the St Joseph, Kankakee and Upper Wabash river basins, with record crests occurring on some rivers. These water levels forced evacuations of homes and closure of businesses and schools, rescues from those driving into flood waters and an overwhelming of water treatment facilities in a few communities. While exact damage figures were not available at the time of entry of the data, preliminary data suggests values into the millions (possibly $10 million or higher).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding was reported around the Tippecanoe River and its associated basin, with several roads suffering damage from partial to full washouts. Damage has been estimated in excess of 250,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought moderate to heavy rain to the region, with reports of one to two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yankee Town bridge on Abington Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East to west oriented MLCAPE axis of 1000 to 2000 J/KG existed across far southern Lower Michigan and far northern Indiana. Effective shear was maximized across northwestern Indiana but did lower somewhat with southward extent. A line of thunderstorms moved across southern Lake Michigan into northwestern Indiana. Additional storms developed further south along theta-e axis. Result was damaging wind gusts, a few which were significant with reports of trees, tree limbs and power lines down in several areas. Localized flooding occurred in some areas, mainly those prone to high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS meteorologist observed a large tree, roughly 10 to 15 inches in diameter was snapped and laid onto County Road E 1100 N.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River rose above flood stage late in the month. December ended up mild and wet. Total precipitation amounts for the month were up to 2.5 inches above normal. Record daily precipitation amounts were noted on the 1st and 14th at several locations. It was the heavy rain at mid-month that caused the river to rise above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Ohio River. Low-lying woods and fields near the river were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers became heavier and more numerous during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of the snow showers reduced visibility to around one mile. The snow accumulated from 1 to 3 inches in many areas. A trained spotter reported three inches of accumulation in Fort Branch in southern Gibson County. A total of 2.5 inches was measured at Santa Claus in Spencer County. Observers in the Community Collaborative Rain, Hail, and Snow (Cocorahs) Network measured two inches in Francisco, Stendal, and Poseyville. One inch fell at Evansville and Boonville. A slushy coating on roads was sufficient to cause some traffic accidents. Traffic accidents were reported on Interstate 64 and U.S. Highway 41. Even after the snow ended, the combination of wet roads from earlier snow and sub-freezing temperatures created patchy slick spots during the early morning commute on the 5th. A couple of counties delayed the opening of schools by two hours. Ahead of high pressure over the Plains states, a moist and unstable northwest wind flow generated numerous snow showers. The snow showers were enhanced by 500 mb shortwaves rotating east around a low over the upper Mississippi Valley.

A narrow line of showers and thunderstorms raced east-northeast across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana, producing pockets of damaging wind and a brief tornado. A deepening low pressure system tracked northeast from Arkansas across the St. Louis area to Indiana. To the east of this low, a warm front lifted north across the lower Ohio Valley in the morning, followed by unseasonably warm and moist air. A trailing cold front then moved east, triggering a narrow line of showers and thunderstorms along the Mississippi River by midday. This line of convection became sporadically severe as it raced across southwest Indiana and western Kentucky in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small number of shingles were blown off a well-constructed house. A few farm outbuildings suffered varying degrees of roof damage, mainly sheets of tin blown off. Several trees were uprooted. This wind damage occurred just prior to the start of a tornado path. The beginning of the tornado path was only 500 yards from the closest damage from this downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of showers and thunderstorms raced east-northeast across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana, producing pockets of damaging wind and a brief tornado. A deepening low pressure system tracked northeast from Arkansas across the St. Louis area to Indiana. To the east of this low, a warm front lifted north across the lower Ohio Valley in the morning, followed by unseasonably warm and moist air. A trailing cold front then moved east, triggering a narrow line of showers and thunderstorms along the Mississippi River by midday. This line of convection became sporadically severe as it raced across southwest Indiana and western Kentucky in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three homes sustained minor damage. The damage was mostly to roofs and fascia. One garage door and a window were blown in. A detached garage was destroyed, with the debris thrown at least 300 yards. An outdoor storage shed was pushed several feet off its foundation. Minor roof damage occurred to at least three metal farm buildings. At least two dozen trees and limbs were snapped or uprooted. Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of showers and thunderstorms raced east-northeast across western Kentucky and southwest Indiana, producing pockets of damaging wind and a brief tornado. A deepening low pressure system tracked northeast from Arkansas across the St. Louis area to Indiana. To the east of this low, a warm front lifted north across the lower Ohio Valley in the morning, followed by unseasonably warm and moist air. A trailing cold front then moved east, triggering a narrow line of showers and thunderstorms along the Mississippi River by midday. This line of convection became sporadically severe as it raced across southwest Indiana and western Kentucky in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of mature trees in a stand of trees along an escarpment were snapped or uprooted. The downed trees were along part of County Road 2160 North. Minor roof damage occurred to a metal farm outbuilding. The damage area was about one-quarter mile long and one-third mile wide. This damage area was only about 600 yards southeast of the end point of a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle developed during the early morning of December 29th and continued through late morning. A light glaze of ice accumulated on many roads causing numerous accidents and spin outs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 year old woman attempted to cross a stream in a car on a slab of concrete. The water was extremely high and fast after heavy rainfall in the area. The car was swept off the slab and the women's body was discovered about 1 1/2 miles down stream the next day. F63VE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms rolled through southern Indiana and central Kentucky during the afternoon hours of December 31, 2018. In addition to strong straight line wind gusts that caused sporadic tree damage in the region, a brief, small EF-1 tornado touched down in Harrison County, Indiana. It caused structural damage to several buildings, as well as uprooting and snapping trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey team determined an intermittent skipping tornado varying in width, size and speed had a maximum wind speed of 100 mph along with a max width of 140 yards.||The tornado initially touched down at 7231 IN-337. At this location, a heavy metal chain fence weighing several hundred pounds was picked up and thrown 30 yards into the back of the house causing significant damage to the house. A chicken house weighing hundreds of pounds was rolled over and moved several feet. A purple Martin house was bent over 60 degrees facing east. Shingles and yard decorations were thrown in various directions east of the house along with driveway gravel thrown in every direction. A swing set was thrown from the back of the house to the front. Throughout the property there were twisted and uprooted trees.||Just before crossing the highway, the tornado increased from 75 yards to 140 yards in width and picked up hundreds of cornstalks tossing them in various directions across the road up to 300 yards away. Across the street at 7768 IN-337 there was significant cedar tree damage along with a 30 by 40 foot barn that was destroyed. The tornado twisted and snapped several cedar trees before going across the farmers field. The tornado narrowed and went to about 50 yards in width.||At 4368 Rogers Campground Road there were several trees twisted and snapped. One single-wide mobile home had siding panels blown out in several spots. An older, well-built barn with sturdy cedar poles sustained significant side panel damage. The metal sheeting was thrown in a northwesterly to northeasterly direction. One 2x4 wood pole was embedded in the ground. The tornado was very narrow along Rogers Campground Road, likely no more than 50 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm system approached from the southwest bringing multiple shots of rain during the morning and afternoon of December 31st. Up to one and a half to two inches of rain fell across central Indiana. Some minor flooding was noted in Decatur County due to heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was reported in the right lane of Interstate 74 near mile marker 137 due to heavy rainfall. One and a half to two inches of rain was reported to have fallen in Decatur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of snow developed in the Ohio Valley, stretching from Central Illinois through southeast Indiana, then through northern Kentucky. Very rapid accumulations occurred, though major troubles on area roadways were inhibited by a relatively warm ground which significantly held back the accumulations on the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official measured 3.2 inches of snow in Batesville. The cooperative observer near Napoleon measured 3 inches of snow. The CoCoRaHS observer 4 miles northeast of Osgood measured 2 inches of snow.

An upper level low brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to central Indiana the evening of November 14 into the early morning hours of November 15. The wintry mix created hazardous travel conditions, caused power outages affecting over 60,000 people (according to Indiana Department of Energy), and caused damage to trees. Most of the ice fell across the eastern two thirds of central Indiana, with light snow across the western third. Ice amounts generally ranged from a tenth of an inch to three-tenths of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple observations of a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation was reported on the southeast side of Bloomington from this storm system. Some tree branches were downed.

The most significant rainfall event of the month happened at the end of October and the beginning of November. Much of central and southern Indiana received 2 to more than 4 inches of rain. Minor flooding quickly followed along the White River by the 3rd. Additional rainfall from one-half to over an inch in southern Indiana fell on the 5th. As a result, the flood crest was prolonged by 2 to 3 days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River. Floodwaters covered low-lying fields and woods near the river, including some low-lying oil fields. A few local rural roads were flooded. High water isolated a small community of river cabins.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River. Bottomland fields and woodlands were flooded. Several county roads were flooded in low-lying areas. A few oil fields were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season bout of winter precipitation caused hazardous conditions. This measurable snow and ice was one of the ten earliest winter events on record for Evansville. The precipitation was mostly snow and sleet west of Evansville, and mostly freezing rain from Evansville eastward. The heaviest precipitation occurred in Spencer County, where around one-quarter inch of ice caused isolated power outages. Around an inch of snow and sleet fell from Evansville and Princeton westward. Roads were covered with snow and ice, including Interstates 64 and 69. Interstate 69 in Gibson County was closed in both directions due to multiple accidents with injuries. The winter precipitation was caused by a strong 500 mb low that moved northeast from Arkansas to the lower Ohio Valley. At the surface, an inverted trough extended from the southeastern states northwest to the lower Ohio Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season bout of winter precipitation caused hazardous conditions. This measurable snow and ice was one of the ten earliest winter events on record for Evansville. The precipitation was mostly snow and sleet west of Evansville, and mostly freezing rain from Evansville eastward. The heaviest precipitation occurred in Spencer County, where around one-quarter inch of ice caused isolated power outages. Around an inch of snow and sleet fell from Evansville and Princeton westward. Roads were covered with snow and ice, including Interstates 64 and 69. Interstate 69 in Gibson County was closed in both directions due to multiple accidents with injuries. The winter precipitation was caused by a strong 500 mb low that moved northeast from Arkansas to the lower Ohio Valley. At the surface, an inverted trough extended from the southeastern states northwest to the lower Ohio Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass already in place combined with an anomalous low pressure system to produce an early season freezing rain event across southern Indiana and central Kentucky.  Many locations received between a tenth and a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation on trees/shrubs and other elevated surfaces.  Warm ground temperatures kept most roads ice free, though some bridges had slick spots.  ||The ice on the trees, many of which still had leaves that helped to weigh them down, caused several limbs/branches to snap and some trees to fall.  There were many reports of power flashes overnight as branches hit transformers.  At the peak of the event, over 100,000 customers were without power in the region. Some lost power for several days. Out of state crews were brought in to help restore power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duke Energy reported approximately 9,600 customers were without power at 6:30 am on the 15th.
